name: N of Lake Livingston
short_description: Public rockhounding area north of Lake Livingston in East Texas offering Quartz, Chalcedony, and Chert specimens. Low elevation terrain suitable for casual collectors.
description:
This site north of Lake Livingston in Trinity County offers accessible rockhounding opportunities in East Texas lowlands. The area sits at only 153 feet elevation in a region dominated by Quaternary alluvial deposits and weathered sedimentary outcrops. Collectors can find Quartz nodules, Chalcedony fragments, and Chert pieces weathering out from local clay and sandstone formations.

The geological setting reflects East Texas's position within the Gulf Coastal Plain, where Tertiary sandstones and siltstones have been deeply weathered and eroded over millions of years. Secondary silica deposits and nodules formed within these sedimentary rocks during diagenesis, creating the material found here today.

Expect a mild, relatively flat terrain typical of East Texas with mixed pine and hardwood cover. The low elevation means hot, humid summers and mild winters. This is a beginner-friendly location for those seeking casual day trips without long hikes or steep grades.

Bring containers for your finds, wear sturdy shoes for uneven ground, and be prepared for humidity. The site is best visited during cooler months to avoid extreme heat. Limited shade is available, so bring sun protection and plenty of water.
history: This region sits within the Gulf Coastal Plain where Tertiary sandstones and siltstones were deposited in shallow marine and deltaic environments. Secondary Quartz and Chalcedony nodules formed within these sedimentary rocks through slow silica precipitation during burial and diagenesis, eventually weathering out as the overlying materials eroded away.
tips: Search along exposed clay banks, eroded stream cuts, and dirt road shoulders where weathering has loosened nodules from the host rock. Look for white, gray, or milky Quartz nodules and translucent tan to brown Chalcedony pieces, typically ranging from thumbnail to fist size. A small pry bar or flathead screwdriver helps extract partially exposed material. Work after recent rains when loose specimens are more visible and ground is stable.
terrain_notes: Elevation 153 feet in flat East Texas lowlands. Terrain is relatively level with mixed pine and hardwood forest cover. Walking distance to productive areas varies but is generally short with uneven footing over roots and loose soil. No significant elevation change or steep grades. Soft ground and low spots may be muddy after rain.
best_season: October - April. East Texas summers are hot and humid (90F+), making field work uncomfortable. Fall through spring offers mild temperatures (50-70F) and lower humidity, ideal for extended rockhounding trips.
written_directions: From Carlisle, head north on local roads toward Lake Livingston State Park area. GPS coordinates 30.87394, -95.211034 place the site north of the lake. Use County Road 3126 or similar county roads to access public land and roadside collecting areas. Parking is available at pullouts and public access areas near the lake region. Confirm access at the lake park office or with county records before visiting.
road_conditions: County roads are typically unpaved or partially paved with regular maintenance. Some washboard conditions possible. Standard passenger vehicles can access most areas, but avoid during heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along county roads and near lake access points. Capacity varies by specific location. No developed parking lot; use discretion and respect private property.
collection_rules: Personal use only on public land. Respect private property boundaries. No motorized excavation. Maximum 25 lbs per day per federal guidelines. Do not damage structures or remove specimens from active erosion areas that endanger stability. Always obtain landowner permission on private land.
quantity_limits: 25 lbs per day for personal use
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect private property
Obtain landowner permission on private land
nearest_services: Carlisle (approximately 8 miles south) offers limited services. Trinity or surrounding towns have gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Uneven footing over roots and loose soil, but no steep grades or significant obstacles. Standard footwear sufficient, though sturdy shoes recommended. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Parking areas are accessible.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find north of Lake Livingston in Texas?
faq.answer: This site yields Quartz nodules, Chalcedony fragments, and Chert pieces. Most specimens are white, gray, milky, or tan to brown in color, ranging from thumbnail to fist size. The area sits within the Gulf Coastal Plain where these secondary silica minerals formed in weathered Tertiary sandstones and siltstones.

faq.question: Is rockhounding near Lake Livingston free and does it require a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding on public land near Lake Livingston is free and does not require a permit for personal use. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day. Always confirm access with county records and respect private property boundaries. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding north of Lake Livingston?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. East Texas summers are extremely hot and humid (often above 90F), making field work uncomfortable and unsafe. Fall through spring offers mild temperatures between 50-70F and lower humidity, making it much more pleasant for extended collecting trips.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at this Lake Livingston location for beginners?
faq.answer: This is an excellent beginner location. The terrain is flat, walking distances are short, and no steep hikes are required. Specimens weather naturally from exposed banks and road shoulders, so minimal digging tools are needed. Families and children ages 6+ can enjoy this accessible site.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to find Quartz and Chalcedony near Carlisle, Texas?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or flathead screwdriver to extract partially exposed nodules, sturdy shoes for uneven ground, containers for specimens, sun protection, and plenty of water. A headlamp is useful for exploring shaded areas. Search along eroded stream cuts, clay banks, and dirt road shoulders where weathering exposes material.

faq.question: Are there hazards I should be aware of when rockhounding near Lake Livingston?
faq.answer: Watch for snakes, especially in warm months (October - April reduces this risk). The area can be hot and humid, so bring adequate water and sun protection. Low spots may be muddy after rain, making parking and access more difficult. Cell service is spotty, so inform someone of your location before heading out.

meta_title: Lake Livingston Rockhounding - Trinity County, Texas
meta_description: Find Quartz, Chalcedony, and Chert north of Lake Livingston in Texas. Free public rockhounding near Carlisle on East Texas public land.
